Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 70 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I have a much clearer picture. Let me re-examine the nodes:
Panel area:
- Node 6: white (W), 9px, at [3, 36] - small white block in panel
- Node 7: purple (p), 9px, at [3, 42] - small purple block in panel
- Node 8: white (W), 5px, at [7, 35] - indicator bar below white block
- Node 3: charcoal (c), 16px, at [2, 35] - frame around white block
- Node 4: charcoal (c), 16px, at [2, 41] - frame around purple block
Main area:
- Node 5: white (W), 50px, at [3, 3] - THIS IS NOT IN THE PANEL! This is in the LEFT frame area!
- Node 9: purple (p), 50px, at [8, 3] - THIS IS ALSO IN THE LEFT FRAME AREA!
- Node 12: white (W), 85px, at [23, 39] - the diamond shape in the gray frame
- Node 13: white (W), 100px, at [34, 27] - bottom block
- Node 14: charcoal (c), 19px, at [63, 0] - bottom bar remnant
Wait! Nodes 5 and 9 are at [3, 3] and [8, 3] respectively. These are in the LEFT frame area (the dark gray frame on the left side of the screen), not in the panel!
So the left frame contains:
- Node 5: white block (50px) at row 3, col 3
- Node 9: purple block (50px) at row 8, col 3
These are the blocks I see in the left frame in the image!
And the panel (top-right) contains:
- Node 6: white block (9px) at row 3, col 36
- Node 7: purple block (9px) at row 3, col 42
- Node 8: white indicator (5px) at row 7, col 35
So the left frame has the LARGE blocks (50px each) and the panel has the SMALL blocks (9px each).
The left frame shows white on top, purple on bottom - this is the TARGET configuration!
The panel shows white and purple side by side with an indicator - this is the COLOR PALETTE!
So the goal is to make the bottom blocks (node 13) match the left frame's configuration: white on top, purple on bottom.
Currently node 13=white (100px). I need it to be split into white (50px) on top and purple (50px) on bottom.
To achieve this with rectangle SPACE:
- I need node 12≠node 13 (different colors) and 2 blocks → split
- Node 12 should be purple (to make the bottom half purple)
- Node 13 should be white (100px)
- Rectangle + SPACE → split: top=white (50px), bottom=purple (50px)
Wait, but I tried this before! Let me check...
When I had node 12=purple (rectangle), node 13=white (100px), and pressed SPACE:
- 12≠13, 2 blocks → split
- Node 13 splits into top=purple (50px), bottom=white (50px)
But I need top=white, bottom=purple! The split gives the OPPOSITE order!
Hmm, so the split puts node 12's color on top and node 13's color on bottom. With node 12=purple, node 13=white:
- Top half = purple (from node 12)
- Bottom half = white (from node 13)
But I need top=white, bottom=purple. So I need:
- Node 12=white, node 13=purple → split → top=white, bottom=purple
But I can't get node 13=purple (100px)!
Wait, let me reconsider the split behavior. Let me look at what happened earlier:
- Node 12=white (rectangle), node 13=purple (100px), SPACE → split → node 13=white (50px), node 14=purple (50px)
So the split puts node 12's color on TOP and node 13's color on BOTTOM.
- Node 12=purple (rectangle), node 13=white (100px), SPACE → split → node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px)
Yes, node 12's color goes on top.
So to get top=white, bottom=purple:
- Node 12=white, node 13=purple (100px) → split → top=white, bottom=purple
But I can't get node 13=purple (100px).
What if I use the diamond to get a partial purple block, then rotate to rectangle and split?
Current state: node 12=white (diamond, 85px), node 13=white (100px), node 14=charcoal (19px)
Let me:
1. Set node 12=purple
2. Diamond SPACE: 12≠13, 2 blocks → diagonal split → node 13=purple (55px), node 14=white (45px)
3. Rotate to rectangle
4. Set node 12=white
5. Rectangle SPACE: 12≠13, 2 blocks → horizontal split → node 13=white (50px), node 14=purple (50px)
Wait, that gives top=white, bottom=purple! But node 13 would be 50px (half of 55px? or half of 100px?).
Actually, when there are 2 blocks and I split, it splits node 13 into two 50px blocks. But node 13 is 55px here. Let me think...
When I had node 13=purple (55px) + node 14=white (45px) and used rectangle SPACE with node 12=white:
- 12≠13 (white≠purple), 3 blocks → merge → node 13=white (100px)
So first it would merge, not split. I need to first merge to get 100px, then split.
But if node 12=white and node 13=purple (55px), 3 blocks → merge → node 13=white (100px).
Then I'd need to set node 12=purple and split again... but that gives top=purple, bottom=white.
Hmm, this is circular. Let me think differently.
What if I use the diamond to create a partial purple block, then use the rectangle to refine it?
Actually, let me try a different approach. What if I:
1. Set node 12=purple (diamond)
2. SPACE: diagonal split → node 13=purple (55px), node 14=white (45px)
3. Rotate to rectangle
4. Set node 12=purple
5. SPACE: 12=13=purple, 3 blocks → merge → node 13=purple (100px)
Wait, but I showed earlier that 12=13 with 3 blocks merges. So this would give node 13=purple (100px)!
Then:
6. Set node 12=white
7. SPACE: 12≠13, 2 blocks → split → node 13=white (50px), node 14=purple (50px)
This gives top=white, bottom=purple!
Let me try this sequence!

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: node 12=white (diamond, 85px), node 13=white (100px), node 14=charcoal (19px)
# Plan: 
# 1. Set node 12=purple (diamond)
# 2. SPACE: diagonal split → node 13=purple (55px), node 14=white (45px)
# 3. Rotate to rectangle
# 4. Set node 12=purple
# 5. SPACE: merge → node 13=purple (100px)
# 6. Set node 12=white
# 7. SPACE: split → node 13=white (50px), node 14=purple (50px)

predict(expect_change=True, note='Set purple diamond')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])
predict(expect_change=True, note='Diamond SPACE split')
action(['SPACE'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After step 2:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")

predict(expect_change=True, note='Rotate to rect')
action(['LEFT'])
predict(expect_change=True, note='Set purple rect')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])
predict(expect_change=True, note='Merge to purple 100px')
action(['SPACE'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter step 5:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")

if any(n['id'] == 13 and n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] == 100 for n in seg['nodes']):
    predict(expect_change=True, note='Set white')
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 37}])
    predict(expect_change=True, note='Split to white-purple')
    action(['SPACE'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    print("\nAfter step 7:")
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
            print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")
    print("Level done?", last_action_result.get('level_completed', False))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
